What the Brain/Helm split changes when you want to own every line of your UI
Every Angular team that sets out to build a real in-house design system runs into the same dilemma.
Start from an already-styled library, and spend weeks undoing it. Or start from scratch, and rewrite accessibility by hand: keyboard navigation, focus management, ARIA states.
Spartan UI answers that dilemma in a way worth stopping for: it refuses to pick a side.
The problem PrimeNG and Material don’t really solve
A library like PrimeNG or Angular Material ships with a full theme, its own CSS variables, and an API designed to work as-is. That’s a real speed boost on day one.
But the moment you want a strong, consistent visual identity across a product, that convenience turns against the team. You end up overriding a theme that was never meant to be overridden, working around generated class names, stacking ::ng-deep on top of an architecture you don't control.
That’s not a flaw in these libraries. They just weren’t built for this: they ship ready-made components, not a foundation you’re meant to own end to end.
The real cost isn’t installing…
